signals.title: NZD/JPY — G10 Bilateral Theme + Carry
signals.text: NZD/JPY trades at 92.34 as the G10 currency scorecard triggers a critical bilateral theme signal. NZD is the strongest G10 currency this week, while JPY remains the clear laggard. This divergence is fundamentally driven by the +127bp OIS carry premium favoring NZD (RBNZ OCR-overnight 2.25% vs BoJ TONA 0.98%) and a hawkish RBNZ market bias heading into the July 8 policy decision. JPY remains heavily pressured by its -0.715% weekly average performance across G10 peers, while NZD outperformance averages +0.635%. The massive 1.351% G10 spread confirms a highly concentrated long-NZD/short-JPY flow. This carry-seeking regime remains dominant while VIX remains at 16.9 (normal volatility), though any sudden risk-off spike will trigger rapid carry compression. Watch the July 8 RBNZ policy decision for confirmation of the hawkish bias.

signals.title: USD/JPY — Positioning + Vol + Retail Divergence
signals.text: USD/JPY trades at 162.69, holding well above the critical 160.00 threshold where verbal intervention risk historically escalates. The options market is actively pricing in downside protection, with the 25d Risk Reversal showing a moderate put skew of -1.23 and USD/JPY implied volatility at 7.2% vs a realized HV30 of 2.4% (generating an elevated +4.8% Vol Risk Premium). This institutional hedging directly contrasts with extreme retail sentiment, where 88% of retail accounts are short USD/JPY, attempting to fade the spot trend. Meanwhile, CFTC speculative positioning remains heavily short JPY (lev_net -115,033), showing that institutional carry players are still collecting the +264bp SOFR-TONA carry advantage. This combination of crowded institutional shorts, extreme retail contrarian positioning, and elevated options hedging indicates that while carry keeps the pair bid, a sudden BoJ intervention will trigger a violent, high-volatility unwind. Watch for verbal warnings from Japanese officials or a spike in VIX above 25 to invalidate the carry-first regime.

signals.title: EUR/USD — Macro + Vol + Positioning
signals.text: EUR/USD trades at 1.1395 following Eurozone June CPI slowing to 2.8% y/y (vs 3.0% expected) and dovish comments from ECB's Wunsch and Nagel, which solidify expectations for an ECB pause. This macro deceleration is supported by a negative EUR Economic Surprise Index (CESI net -9), contrasting with the highly positive US CESI (net +291). Speculators are already positioned for this divergence, with EUR CFTC positioning net short at -67,504 contracts. The options market is actively hedging downside risk, with EUR/USD ATM IV at 7.1% vs HV30 at 4.6% (a +2.5% Vol Risk Premium) and a mild put skew of -0.44 on the 25d Risk Reversal. The +150bp nominal carry advantage (Fed 3.75% vs ECB 2.25%) and a restrictive US real rate of +1.56% vs EUR's +0.08% structurally limit EUR/USD upside. Watch the July 23 ECB meeting and tomorrow's US Non-Farm Payrolls for the next directional catalyst.

signals.title: GBP/USD — Volatility Premium + CESI Alignment
signals.text: GBP/USD trades at 1.3250 amid an exceptionally high options volatility premium. GBP/USD implied volatility stands at 11.0% against a realized HV30 of 5.3%, creating a massive +5.7% Vol Risk Premium—the highest in the G10 space. This elevated premium indicates intense hedging demand ahead of US NFP and the July 30 BoE meeting. Speculative positioning remains virtually flat (CFTC lev_net +631, 50.3% long), but the positive GBP Economic Surprise Index (CESI net +22) and a mildly restrictive real policy rate of +0.78% provide structural support. The options market shows a moderate put skew of -0.80 on the 25d Risk Reversal, indicating that despite solid domestic data, institutional players are paying up for downside protection against a stronger US Dollar. Watch the US NFP release on July 2 to confirm if the USD-driven put skew materializes.

signals.title: USD/CAD — Cross-Asset Correlation Break
signals.text: USD/CAD trades at 1.4219, exhibiting a significant cross-asset correlation break against WTI crude oil. The rolling 30-day correlation between USD/CAD and WTI stands at -0.833, representing a -1.94σ deviation from the 252-day historical norm of -0.032. This extreme negative correlation indicates that the traditional CAD-oil link has decoupled, with USD/CAD price action becoming entirely dominated by the +150bp Fed-BoC carry advantage (Fed 3.75% vs BoC 2.25%) and diverging economic data (US CESI +291 vs CAD CESI -6). Speculators are heavily short CAD (CFTC lev_net -87,096), while retail sentiment is at an extreme 94% short USD/CAD, setting up a potential short-squeeze risk if US data continues to outperform. Watch the BoC policy meeting on July 15 to see if a dovish shift validates further CAD weakness.
